shortcut: Drop -en from the infinitive. That's the command form. Same as the ik-stem.
explanation: The imperative is the easiest verb form in Dutch — it's literally the verb stem (the same form you use after 'ik'). Use it for direct instructions, recipes, signs, and polite requests when softened with 'alstublieft' or 'even'.

patterns.rule: Form — drop the -en from the infinitive
examples: komen → Kom! (Come!)
gaan → Ga! (Go!)
eten → Eet! (Eat!)
drinken → Drink! (Drink!)
bellen → Bel! (Call!)
studeren → Studeer! (Study!) ← long-vowel rule: e doubles to ee
hoesten → Hoest! (Cough!)
kijken → Kijk! (Look!)
luisteren → Luister! (Listen!)
↳ Same spelling rules as the ik-stem: double the vowel for long-vowel verbs, drop the doubled letter for short ones.

patterns.rule: Softening — Belgian Dutch loves to soften commands
examples: Kom alstublieft binnen. (Please come in.) ← alstublieft = please (formal)
Kom even hier. (Come here for a moment.) ← 'even' softens any command
Wacht eens. (Wait a moment.) ← 'eens' = just / for once
Wacht maar. (Just wait.) ← 'maar' = just (reassuring)
Kijk eens naar dit. (Just look at this.)
↳ Bare imperatives sound abrupt in Belgian Dutch. Adding 'even', 'eens', 'maar', or 'alstublieft' makes them polite without changing the verb.
Snijd de groenten klein. (Cut the vegetables small.)
Kook de pasta tien minuten. (Cook the pasta for ten minutes.)
Voeg zout en peper toe. (Add salt and pepper.)
Open hier. (Open here.) — on packaging
Niet roken. (No smoking.) — infinitive used as instruction on signs
Vul het formulier in. (Fill in the form.) — separable, prefix at end

patterns.rule: Separable verbs — prefix goes to the end
examples: opstaan → Sta op! (Get up!)
uitgaan → Ga uit! (Go out!)
aandoen → Doe aan! (Put on!) ← Doe je jas aan! (Put your coat on!)
thuiskomen → Kom thuis! (Come home!)
↳ Same split rule as in main clauses — the prefix is the LAST word.
Komt u binnen. (Please come in.) ← stem + t for the formal 'u'
Wacht u even, alstublieft. (Please wait a moment.)
Neemt u plaats. (Please take a seat.)
↳ For very formal contexts (forms, official requests), Dutch uses stem + t. The bare-stem form is fine in most everyday Belgian contexts.

memory: 🧠 Imperative = ik-stem with an exclamation point. Same form, different intent. 'Kom!' = 'ik kom' with attitude. Always soften in Belgian Dutch with 'even', 'eens', 'maar' or 'alstublieft' — bare commands sound rude.

examples.nl: Kom op tijd naar de school.

examples.en: Study hard for the exam. (long-vowel rule: ee)
Put your clothes on. (separable — 'aan' at the end)

practice.nl: Sta op! (separable — 'op' goes to the end)
practice.en: Sta op! (separable — 'op' goes to the end)

practice.nl: Kun je dit aan mij geven?
practice.en: Can you give this to me?

practice.nl: Geef het maar. (Just give it.) ← softened with 'maar'
